sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: An analytical chemistry lab separates three compounds by reverse-phase HPLC using a nonpolar stationary phase and a polar mobile phase. Compound A elutes at 2.0 min, Compound B at 5.0 min, and Compound C at 8.0 min. A UV detector records peak areas proportional to amount. Peak areas are 100, 200, and 50 arbitrary units for A, B, and C, respectively. Students interpret retention time based on hydrophobic interactions with the stationary phase.
questions.stem: Which compound is most strongly retained on the reverse-phase column?

explanations.B: Correct. In reverse-phase HPLC, longer retention time indicates stronger interaction with the nonpolar stationary phase. Compound C elutes latest at 8.0 min.
explanations.A: Compound A elutes first and is least retained.
explanations.C: Compound B has an intermediate retention time.
explanations.D: The retention times are not equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Retention times and UV peak areas for three compounds separated by reverse-phase HPLC.

questions.stem: Which compound is present in the greatest amount based on peak area?

explanations.C: Correct. Peak area is proportional to amount. Compound B has the largest area, 200 arbitrary units.
explanations.A: Compound A has a smaller peak area of 100.
explanations.B: Compound C has the smallest peak area of 50.
explanations.D: A and C are not equal in peak area.

questions.stem: In reverse-phase HPLC, a more hydrophobic compound generally has what retention behavior?

explanations.A: Correct. A hydrophobic compound interacts more strongly with the nonpolar stationary phase, so it is retained longer before eluting.
explanations.B: Shorter retention would be expected for more polar compounds in reverse phase.
explanations.C: Hydrophobic compounds can be retained strongly rather than not at all.
explanations.D: Peak area is not negative due to hydrophobicity.

questions.stem: What would increasing the fraction of nonpolar solvent in the mobile phase generally do?

choices.D: Decrease retention times for hydrophobic compounds
choices.B: Make the detector stop absorbing light

explanations.D: Correct. A stronger nonpolar mobile phase competes better for hydrophobic analytes in reverse-phase HPLC, often causing them to elute sooner.
explanations.A: Retention does not increase without limit when mobile phase becomes better at carrying hydrophobic solutes.
explanations.B: Solvent composition does not inherently stop UV detection.
explanations.C: Changing solvent composition does not transform the method into electrophoresis.

questions.stem: Why is retention time not by itself a complete measure of amount?

choices.C: Retention time identifies when a compound elutes, whereas peak area reflects quantity
choices.A: Retention time always equals molar mass
choices.B: Peak area is unrelated to detector response
choices.D: All compounds with long retention have the same concentration

explanations.C: Correct. Retention time helps identify or compare compounds, but amount is estimated from peak area after appropriate calibration. The two quantities report different information.
explanations.A: Retention time depends on interactions with the column and mobile phase, not simply molar mass.
explanations.B: Peak area is explicitly proportional to amount in the passage.
explanations.D: Long retention does not imply equal concentration.

questions.passage: A radiation physics demonstration measures gamma-ray intensity after shielding with lead sheets. Initial intensity without shielding is 1000 counts/s. After 1, 2, 3, and 4 half-value layers, intensities are 500, 250, 125, and 62.5 counts/s. Students use the exponential attenuation model I = I0(1/2)n, where n is the number of half-value layers. Background count rate is small compared with the measured values.
questions.stem: What intensity remains after 3 half-value layers?

explanations.B: Correct. Each half-value layer halves intensity. After 3 layers, 1000 × (1/2)³ = 125 counts/s.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Gamma-ray intensity decreases by half for each added half-value layer of lead.

questions.stem: What fraction of the original intensity remains after 4 half-value layers?

explanations.D: Correct. Four half-value layers reduce intensity by (1/2)^4 = 1/16, matching 62.5/1000.

questions.stem: Why does shielding reduce measured intensity?

choices.A: Photons are absorbed or scattered out of the beam
choices.B: Lead converts all gamma rays into electrons that continue straight through
choices.D: The source activity increases behind the shield

explanations.A: Correct. Shielding attenuates radiation by absorption and scattering, so fewer photons reach the detector along the beam path.
explanations.B: Interactions may produce secondary particles, but not all gamma rays simply continue straight through as electrons.
explanations.C: The detector is not becoming more radioactive in this attenuation measurement.
explanations.D: Adding shielding does not increase source activity.

questions.stem: If background count were not negligible, what correction would be needed?

choices.B: Subtract background counts from measured counts before analyzing attenuation
choices.A: Add background counts to the initial intensity only
choices.D: Multiply all counts by the number of layers

explanations.B: Correct. Background contributes to measured counts independently of the source beam. Subtracting it gives the net radiation intensity from the source.
explanations.A: Background should be corrected consistently, not only for the initial reading.
explanations.C: Shielded measurements still contain useful information after correction.
explanations.D: Multiplying by layers is not a background correction.

questions.stem: How many half-value layers are needed to reduce intensity below 10% of the original?

explanations.C: Correct. After 3 layers, 1/8 = 12.5% remains, still above 10%. After 4 layers, 1/16 = 6.25% remains, below 10%.
explanations.D: Three layers leave 12.5%, which is still above 10%.
